era_context: The High Middle Ages; the Byzantine Empire under Constantine X Doukas faces continued pressure from Seljuk Turks and other frontier threats; the Song Dynasty in China is in its mature phase; the Fatimid Caliphate in Egypt is declining; Norman expansion in southern Italy and Sicily continues; the Holy Roman Empire is in the reign of Henry IV.

events.title: Kidnapping of Henry IV of the Holy Roman Empire; the Kaiserswerth Incident
events.description: In April 1062, the young King Henry IV of the Holy Roman Empire (then about 12 years old) was kidnapped by Archbishop Anno II of Cologne in what became known as the Kaiserswerth Incident. Anno II, acting as regent, removed Henry from the influence of his mother, the Empress Agnes, and took control of the young king. This event was a turning point in German politics, as it shifted power away from the imperial court and toward the German princes and the Church. The kidnapping was justified as protecting the young king from his mother's allegedly poor governance, but it was fundamentally a power grab by Anno II. The incident had long-term consequences for the development of German feudalism and the relationship between the crown and the German princes.
key_figures: Henry IV (King of the Holy Roman Empire, age ~12)

sources.name: Lampert of Hersfeld, Annales (written late 11th century, near-contemporary account)
Historiographic consensus on the Kaiserswerth Incident

events.certainty_note: The kidnapping of Henry IV in April 1062 is well-attested in medieval sources, particularly Lampert of Hersfeld's Annales, which was written within a generation of the event. The date and basic facts are confirmed.
cross_references: 1075-001 (Investiture Controversy, related to Henry IV's later conflicts with the Church)

events.title: Norman consolidation in southern Italy; continued expansion of Norman power
events.description: In 1062, Norman forces under Robert Guiscard and other Norman leaders continued their consolidation of power in southern Italy. The Normans had been expanding their territory in Apulia and Calabria for decades, and by 1062 they controlled much of southern Italy. This year saw continued military campaigns and the strengthening of Norman administrative control. The Norman conquest of southern Italy was a major geopolitical shift, as it brought the region under the control of a new European power and eventually led to the establishment of the Kingdom of Sicily.

sources.name: Geoffrey Malaterra, De rebus gestis Rogerii Comitis
William of Apulia, Gesta Roberti Wiscardi

events.certainty_note: Norman expansion in southern Italy during the 1060s is well-established, but specific events for 1062 are not precisely documented. The broader pattern is confirmed.

events.title: Byzantine-Seljuk tensions escalate; military pressure on Anatolia
events.description: In 1062, tensions between the Byzantine Empire and the Seljuk Turks continued to escalate. The Seljuks, under Alp Arslan, were conducting raids and military campaigns into Byzantine Anatolia, gradually conquering territory. The Byzantine Empire, under Constantine X Doukas, was struggling to respond effectively to the Seljuk threat due to internal political challenges and limited military resources. The year 1062 falls within a period of increasing Seljuk pressure that would culminate in the Battle of Manzikert in 1071.
events.certainty_note: Seljuk pressure on Anatolia in the 1060s is well-established, but specific events for 1062 are not precisely dated. The broader pattern is confirmed.

events.title: Pope Alexander II's papacy; reform efforts and church-state relations
events.description: Pope Alexander II (r. 1061–1073) was in the early years of his papacy in 1062. He was a reformer who sought to strengthen papal authority and promote ecclesiastical reform, particularly regarding clerical celibacy and the elimination of simony (the sale of church offices). Alexander II's papacy was marked by efforts to assert papal independence from secular rulers and to reform the Church from within. His reign was a precursor to the more dramatic reforms of Pope Gregory VII, who would succeed him.
events.certainty_note: Pope Alexander II's papacy and his reform efforts are well-documented in papal records and medieval chronicles. The date of his papacy (1061–1073) is confirmed.
cross_references: 1073-001 (Accession of Pope Gregory VII, continuation of reform efforts)

disconfirming_evidence: The Kaiserswerth Incident is sometimes presented as a clear-cut power grab by Anno II, but some modern scholarship suggests that the situation was more complex. Empress Agnes's regency may have been genuinely problematic, and Anno II's actions, while self-serving, may have been justified by concerns about the governance of the young king. The incident is not simply a story of villainous ambition but rather a reflection of the complex power dynamics of the Holy Roman Empire in the 11th century. Additionally, the long-term significance of the incident is sometimes overstated; while it was important, it was one of many factors that shaped German political development.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1062 is moderate. The Kaiserswerth Incident is well-attested in near-contemporary chronicles, particularly Lampert of Hersfeld's Annales. Norman expansion in southern Italy is described in later chronicles written in the late 11th century. Byzantine-Seljuk relations are described in later Byzantine sources. Papal records for Alexander II's papacy are reasonably well-preserved. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward Western European and Mediterranean events; events in Africa, Central Asia, East Asia, and the Americas are absent from surviving written sources. Islamic sources for this year exist but are underrepresented in this summary.

graph_edges.note: The Kaiserswerth Incident and Anno II's regency shaped Henry IV's later conflicts with the Church, including the Investiture Controversy
Norman consolidation in southern Italy in 1062 led to the conquest of Palermo in 1063
Seljuk pressure in 1062 escalated to the Battle of Manzikert in 1071
